graph.topic: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
graph.researcher_bg: An HCI researcher skilled in system design who seeks structured data but lacks any clinical experience.
graph.expert_bg: A frontline occupational therapist who relies heavily on tacit clinical intuition to manage complex and unpredictable patient interactions.

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

q_01__exp_L1_00.label: Frontline Occupational Therapist
q_01__exp_L1_00.description: Tacit clinical intuition used to manage complex and unpredictable patient interactions during cognitive assessments.

q_01__exp_L2_00_00.label: Environmental Friction & Ward Dynamics
q_01__exp_L2_00_00.description: How the physical layout and social hustle of the ward create barriers to clean assessment data.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Alarm Noise Breaking Focus
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: You know that moment when the IV pump starts beeping right as you ask them to remember the address? It's gone.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Privacy & Location Constraints
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Trying to find a quiet corner, but sometimes you're stuck at the bedside with the curtain open and the guy next bed watching.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Workflow & Visitor Interruptions
q_01__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Nurse comes in to do obs or a visitor starts talking right when you're getting to the hard questions.

q_01__exp_L2_00_01.label: Timing & Patient State
q_01__exp_L2_00_01.description: Managing the patient's physiological and emotional readiness for testing.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Chasing the Medication Window
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: You learn to catch them when the pain meds have kicked in but before the afternoon nap hits.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety
q_01__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: You can feel their hands shaking or see them shrinking back before you even pull out the test sheet.

q_01__exp_L2_00_02.label: Real-Time Adaptation & Validity
q_01__exp_L2_00_02.description: Judging whether the score reflects true cognitive ability and adjusting the process to ensure accuracy.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Modifying Pace & Phrasing
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: They're getting frustrated, so you tweak the questions or slow down, even if the test says don't.

q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Intuition vs. Documentation
q_01__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: You know what their score really means based on the vibe, but it's hard to put that nuance into the official report.

q_01__shared_root.label: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
q_01__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

q_01__res_L1_00.label: Spatial-Physical Interaction Context
q_01__res_L1_00.description: Examines the physical artifacts, spatial arrangements, and environmental constraints that frame the assessment interaction.

q_01__res_L2_00_00.label: Physical Artifact Ecology
q_01__res_L2_00_00.description: Investigates the tools and physical objects used during the assessment and their spatial relationship to the user.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify essential vs. burdensome physical tools
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which physical artifacts (clipboards, timers, pens) are critical for the workflow versus those that create cognitive or physical friction during administration.

q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Map spatial positioning and proxemics
q_01__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Understand how the therapist positions themselves relative to the patient and family members to optimize communication without causing distress.

q_01__res_L2_00_01.label: Environmental Constraints
q_01__res_L2_00_01.description: Analyzes how the physical ward setting dictates the feasibility of conducting assessments.

q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Quantify environmental dictates on location
q_01__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Explore how noise levels, lighting, or ward traffic specifically determine where an assessment can physically take place within the hospital.

q_01__res_L1_01.label: Workflow Temporal Structure
q_01__res_L1_01.description: Focuses on the sequence of actions, timing, and information flow before, during, and after the assessment session.

q_01__res_L2_01_00.label: Pre-Post Transition Flow
q_01__res_L2_01_00.description: Examines the information seeking and handoff steps surrounding the core assessment activity.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Analyze pre-session information retrieval
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Identify what patient data is retrieved immediately before entering the space and what critical information is often missing at that stage.

q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Define post-session immediate actions
q_01__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Document the specific administrative or clinical actions taken immediately after the assessment concludes before the therapist leaves the area.

q_01__res_L2_01_01.label: In-Session Documentation Load
q_01__res_L2_01_01.description: Investigates the mechanics of recording data while simultaneously engaging with the patient.

q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Assess real-time notation vs. recall friction
q_01__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Evaluate the cognitive cost of switching attention between the patient and recording tools during the task versus relying on memory.

q_01__res_L1_02.label: External Social & Environmental Interference
q_01__res_L1_02.description: Explores how external actors and ward rhythms disrupt or modify the standard assessment workflow.

q_01__res_L2_02_00.label: Ward Dynamics & Interruptions
q_01__res_L2_02_00.description: Focuses on interruptions from staff or bystanders distinct from patient behavioral states.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Measure staff interruption frequency and recovery
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Determine how often external staff interruptions occur and what protocols exist for resuming the assessment thread without losing data.

q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Evaluate privacy constraints from bystanders
q_01__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Understand how the presence of family members or other patients in shared spaces alters the standard administration script due to privacy concerns.

q_01__res_L2_02_01.label: Temporal Environmental Suitability
q_01__res_L2_02_01.description: Analyzes how ward schedules and rhythms influence the timing of assessments.

q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify systematic avoidance periods
q_01__res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Discover specific times of day or ward conditions where assessments are systematically avoided due to environmental unsuitability.

analogy.target_concept: Losing your train of thought

structural_mapping.inputs: Critical moment in a task (e.g., recalling address)
structural_mapping.logic: Sudden external intrusion occurs (e.g., noise/visitor)
structural_mapping.outputs: Mental thread snaps, data/task validity lost

analogy.explanation: The researcher treats noise as a static room feature you select beforehand, but the expert experiences it like losing your train of thought: a sudden event that breaks concentration mid-task, making the initial location choice irrelevant.

assistance_payload.scenario: You might find the quietest corner on the ward, but does an IV pump still ruin the assessment right while the patient is answering?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
Assess real-time notation vs. recall friction

conflict.researcher_branch: Assess real-time notation vs. recall friction

analogy.target_concept: Losing your place while dialing a phone number

structural_mapping.inputs: Information held in mind + Competing stimulus (noise or action)
structural_mapping.logic: Brain switches to new stimulus, old thought drops out
structural_mapping.outputs: Original information is lost and must be retrieved again

analogy.explanation: The researcher suspects writing notes does this to you: just as a voice wipes out the phone number you are dialing, the pen might wipe out your track of the assessment, mirroring the patient's experience with the alarm.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you pause to write their score, do you feel your own train of thought break the same way the patient's does when the IV pump starts beeping?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
Measure staff interruption frequency and recovery

conflict.researcher_branch: Measure staff interruption frequency and recovery

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes how environmental alarms, such as IV pumps, disrupt patient focus during cognitive assessments. This concern highlights a critical source of data loss within the clinical workflow.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on machine noise overlooks human-induced interruptions that similarly fragment the assessment process. Without capturing staff interactions, the analysis of workflow friction remains incomplete.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to quantify the frequency of staff interruptions and identify protocols for resuming assessment threads. This data is necessary to map the full spectrum of external interference.
pivot.compelling_reason: Addressing both machine and staff interruptions provides a comprehensive view of environmental support needs. Ignoring staff interactions would underestimate the total cognitive load placed on the therapist.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While those alarm noises are definitely disruptive, I also need to understand how often colleagues interrupt you and what steps you take to get back on track, since both factors impact the assessment's reliability.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

analogy.target_concept: Taking a private call in a shared office

structural_mapping.inputs: Sensitive task + Unwanted audience nearby
structural_mapping.logic: Audience presence invalidates the current space
structural_mapping.outputs: Relocate to ensure confidentiality

analogy.explanation: This shows the researcher that social presence acts like physical noise: both are environmental inputs that force a location change, validating the expert's privacy constraint as a quantifiable dictate.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you pull the curtain closed for an assessment, is it to block the ward noise, or to stop the next patient from watching you?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the significant environmental friction caused by privacy and location constraints during assessments. This highlights the reality that therapists often lack control over their physical setting.

pivot.limitation: If the discussion stays only on the expert's environmental constraints, the researcher misses how therapists adapt their physical positioning within those limited spaces.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand the spatial positioning and proxemics used to optimize communication even when ideal conditions are unavailable.
pivot.compelling_reason: Understanding these micro-adjustments is crucial because it reveals how clinical goals are met despite the limitations the expert faces.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that privacy is often out of your control, but even in those tight spots, how you position yourself relative to the patient can still impact how well the assessment goes.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert is emphasizing the frustration of environmental failures, specifically the lack of private locations for conducting assessments. This highlights the physical barriers that create immediate workflow friction.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on the expert's location constraints misses the critical data on how clinical protocols are modified in real-time to protect patient privacy.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand how the presence of bystanders forces changes to the standard administration script during the assessment.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's environmental struggle is critical, the researcher's concern regarding workflow adaptation is also important for identifying specific support needs.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I completely understand that finding a quiet corner is a major struggle, and we need to document that failure, but could you also walk me through how you change what you actually say or do when you are stuck at the bedside with people watching?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the immediate difficulty of securing privacy at the bedside, such as dealing with open curtains and nearby patients. This highlights the critical spatial friction encountered during cognitive assessments.

pivot.limitation: If the discussion remains fixed on the expert's spatial constraints, it misses the temporal patterns that dictate when suitable spaces are actually available.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher is trying to understand if there are systematic times of day or ward conditions where assessments are consistently avoided due to environmental unsuitability.
pivot.compelling_reason: While the expert's location concerns are valid, the researcher's focus on timing is also important to identify systemic workflow bottlenecks.
pivot.coarse_scenario: You've clearly outlined the spatial challenges, but I also need to know if there are specific times of day when you systematically avoid assessments because no suitable space is available.

choices: Resume exactly where you left off

probes.question: When interrupted during a sensitive question, do you trust the patient's answer:

When you are tracking multiple symptoms, does an interruption cause you to lose:

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the interruption occurred during Routine vitals instead of Sensitive history taking, would your judgment about 'Workflow & Visitor Interruptions' change?

If the data confidence changed from High to Compromised, would your judgment about 'Workflow & Visitor Interruptions' change?

aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

analogy.target_concept: Losing your count during mental math

structural_mapping.inputs: High-focus task + Unexpected social input
structural_mapping.logic: Attention shifts -> Working memory clears
structural_mapping.outputs: Task validity lost (need to restart)

analogy.explanation: While the researcher expects a privacy rule, the expert's concept behaves like losing your count during mental math; the interruption breaks the process validity, not just the confidentiality.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a relative chats during the memory test, do you pause because they might hear private info, or because the patient loses their focus and the result isn't accurate?

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the immediate challenge of managing unexpected interruptions like nurse observations or visitor conversations during cognitive assessments. This reactive workflow management is crucial for understanding real-time resilience.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on handling interruptions as they occur misses the potential systemic patterns where assessments are preemptively avoided due to environmental unsuitability.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to identify specific times of day or ward conditions where assessments are systematically postponed or cancelled to prevent environmental interference.
pivot.compelling_reason: While managing interruptions is vital, understanding avoidance patterns reveals opportunities for proactive environmental control that could reduce the need for reactive adaptation.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that handling interruptions when they happen is critical, but I also need to know if there are specific times you avoid scheduling these assessments altogether because the environment is just too unpredictable.

aligned_path_expert: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the critical necessity of aligning assessments with the patient's medication cycle to ensure optimal cognitive readiness. This physiological timing is recognized as a primary driver for successful evaluation outcomes.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on patient physiology overlooks external ward factors that may render even a medicated patient unavailable due to environmental chaos.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to map systematic avoidance periods caused by environmental unsuitability, such as noise or traffic, independent of patient state.
pivot.compelling_reason: Understanding environmental constraints ensures that the ideal medication window does not coincide with unavoidable ward disruptions, preventing wasted clinical resources.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While catching that medication window is crucial, you likely encounter times when the ward environment itself makes assessment impossible, regardless of how ready the patient is. Capturing those environmental blocks helps us support your scheduling better.

analogy.target_concept: Getting nervous when a teacher hands out tests

structural_mapping.inputs: Visual cue of evaluation material (test sheet / exam papers)
structural_mapping.logic: Material signals judgment or consequence before interaction begins
structural_mapping.outputs: Physical stress response (shaking / tension) prior to task start

analogy.explanation: The researcher sees the clipboard as a neutral tool causing physical friction during use, but like a student seeing exam papers, the expert knows the sheet itself signals judgment, triggering anxiety before any writing starts.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you pull out the assessment form, do you see the patient shrink back before you write anything, making the tool feel like a threat rather than just a helper?

aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

probes.question: Is the patient's readiness compromised more by cognitive overload, physical fatigue, or sensory sensitivity?
Which underlying emotion drives the withdrawal: fear of judgment, fear of failure, or confusion?
Does established history weigh more than immediate warmth, or does professional distance matter most?
Which physical cue signals critical anxiety: hand tremors, shallow breathing, or facial tension?
When they shrink back, is it a reaction to your height, your distance, or your angle?
Does anxiety peak when you mention the test, when you reach for the sheet, or when you hand it over?
Which environmental factor exacerbates anxiety most: bright lighting, background noise, or cramped seating?
Does the presence of a family member typically buffer anxiety, increase pressure, or have no effect?
To reduce tension, should you sit adjacent, sit opposite, or stand slightly away?
Which verbal adjustment helps most: lowering volume, slowing pace, or reducing question complexity?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the patient's cognitive load changed from low to high, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the primary emotion changed from fear of failure to fear of judgment, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the established history changed from none to long-term, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the physical cue changed from hand tremors to shallow breathing, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the reaction changed from leaning away to crossing arms, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the spike timing changed from mentioning the test to handing over the sheet, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the lighting changed from bright to dim, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the family presence changed from a spouse to no one, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If your position changed from sitting adjacent to standing slightly away, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?
If the verbal pace changed from normal to slow, would your judgment about 'Reading Pre-Assessment Anxiety' change?

aligned_path_researcher: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes that modifying pace and phrasing is essential for managing patient frustration and maintaining assessment validity.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on emotional regulation overlooks how external environmental factors, such as bystanders, also compel script deviations.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to understand how privacy constraints and ward dynamics influence the administration of standard protocols.
pivot.compelling_reason: Acknowledging both internal patient needs and external privacy pressures provides a complete view of the barriers affecting clinical workflow.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While adapting to frustration is vital, you also need to consider how bystanders force script changes, since privacy rules might limit what you can ask even if the patient is calm.

conflict.researcher_branch: Identify essential vs. burdensome physical tools

analogy.target_concept: Cooking by timer vs. tasting for doneness

structural_mapping.inputs: Protocol tools (timers, clipboards) map to recipe instructions and kitchen timers
structural_mapping.logic: Strict adherence to tools maps to following the timer blindly; adapting pace maps to tasting the food
structural_mapping.outputs: Valid clinical score maps to a properly cooked meal; efficient workflow maps to fast preparation

analogy.explanation: The researcher defines 'burden' as physical awkwardness (holding the timer), but the expert defines it as outcome risk (burning the meal). Just as a chef ignores a timer to taste the food, the expert modifies the protocol to preserve the patient's state, making the tool burdensome only when it threatens validity.

assistance_payload.scenario: When a stopwatch makes a patient shut down, do you discard it because it's awkward to hold, or because it ruins the rapport you need to measure their true ability?

analogy.target_concept: Fitting a complex story into a text message

structural_mapping.inputs: Patient's nuanced performance cues
structural_mapping.logic: Compressing observation into fixed fields
structural_mapping.outputs: Official clinical report

analogy.explanation: The researcher assumes the 'tool' is physical, but like hitting a text limit, your burden is the form's vocabulary constraints, showing the friction is about language, not physical handling.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you know a score misses the mark, is the real friction holding the pen, or finding the right words in the form to explain what you actually saw?

analogy.target_concept: Describing a meal's taste using only the ingredient list

structural_mapping.inputs: Patient's actual performance and effort
structural_mapping.logic: Translating holistic observation into structured scores
structural_mapping.outputs: Official report lacking nuance

analogy.explanation: Both face a record-keeping gap: the researcher sees it as stopping to write the list (attention), while the expert sees it as the list missing the taste (meaning).

assistance_payload.scenario: When you write the report, is the hardest part remembering what they did, or finding the words to describe what you sensed about their effort?

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes how environmental alarms, such as IV pumps, disrupt patient focus during cognitive assessments. This concern highlights a critical source of data loss within the clinical workflow.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on machine noise overlooks human-induced interruptions that similarly fragment the assessment process. Without capturing staff interactions, the analysis of workflow friction remains incomplete.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to quantify the frequency of staff interruptions and identify protocols for resuming assessment threads. This data is necessary to map the full spectrum of external interference.
pivot.compelling_reason: Addressing both machine and staff interruptions provides a comprehensive view of environmental support needs. Ignoring staff interactions would underestimate the total cognitive load placed on the therapist.
pivot.coarse_scenario: While those alarm noises are definitely disruptive, I also need to understand how often colleagues interrupt you and what steps you take to get back on track, since both factors impact the assessment's reliability.

assistance_payload.scenario: When you pull the curtain closed for an assessment, is it to block the ward noise, or to stop the next patient from watching you?

pivot.compelling_reason: Understanding these micro-adjustments is crucial because it reveals how clinical goals are met despite the limitations the expert faces.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that privacy is often out of your control, but even in those tight spots, how you position yourself relative to the patient can still impact how well the assessment goes.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: The expert emphasizes the immediate challenge of managing unexpected interruptions like nurse observations or visitor conversations during cognitive assessments. This reactive workflow management is crucial for understanding real-time resilience.

pivot.limitation: Focusing solely on handling interruptions as they occur misses the potential systemic patterns where assessments are preemptively avoided due to environmental unsuitability.
pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to identify specific times of day or ward conditions where assessments are systematically postponed or cancelled to prevent environmental interference.
pivot.compelling_reason: While managing interruptions is vital, understanding avoidance patterns reveals opportunities for proactive environmental control that could reduce the need for reactive adaptation.
pivot.coarse_scenario: I understand that handling interruptions when they happen is critical, but I also need to know if there are specific times you avoid scheduling these assessments altogether because the environment is just too unpredictable.

pivot.research_goal: The researcher aims to map systematic avoidance periods caused by environmental unsuitability, such as noise or traffic, independent of patient state.

pivot.coarse_scenario: While catching that medication window is crucial, you likely encounter times when the ward environment itself makes assessment impossible, regardless of how ready the patient is. Capturing those environmental blocks helps us support your scheduling better.

q_02__exp_L1_00.label: Frontline Occupational Therapist
q_02__exp_L1_00.description: Tacit clinical intuition used to manage complex and unpredictable patient interactions during cognitive assessments.

q_02__exp_L2_00_00.label: Detecting the Breakpoint
q_02__exp_L2_00_00.description: Recognizing the subtle, often non-verbal signs that a patient is transitioning from focused to agitated or withdrawn during the test.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: The 'Thousand-Yard Stare'
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: You see their eyes lose focus and fixate on nothing while you're asking them to count backwards; they're still there physically, but cognitively they've checked out.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Restless Hands & Breathing
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: You notice their breathing gets faster or they start fidgeting with their hands more than usual; it's a physical sign of rising panic before they speak.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Sharpness in Tone
q_02__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: They snap at you or their voice gets flat, clipped, or higher over a simple question—you know it's not anger at you, but panic masking as irritation.

q_02__exp_L2_00_01.label: Tactical Pivots
q_02__exp_L2_00_01.description: Immediate, in-the-moment strategies used to de-escalate tension and regain cooperation without invalidating the assessment.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: The 'Side-Step' Conversation
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: You deliberately drop the test questions for two minutes to ask about their grandkids or day, just to reset the vibe before trying the hard stuff again.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Physical Reset
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Offering a glass of water, shifting your chair back, or just taking a breath to let them reset; changing the physical dynamic breaks the tension loop.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Simplifying Instructions
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Breaking down a complex command into single steps without actually giving them the answer, just to keep them moving forward without overwhelm.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Skipping the Hard Bit
q_02__exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: You know if you push the serial 7s now they'll walk out, so you skip it and come back later if they calm down, even if protocol says sequential.

q_02__exp_L2_00_02.label: Validity & Safety Trade-offs
q_02__exp_L2_00_02.description: Judging whether the data collected is usable and determining the threshold where patient well-being outweighs assessment completion.

q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: Distinguishing Deficit vs. Distress
q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: Trying to figure out if they failed because they can't remember, or because they were too upset to try—that distinction changes the whole care plan.
aliases: lose the data if they shut down

q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Knowing When to Abort
q_02__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: There's a gut feeling when their breathing gets too shallow or agitation spikes—you just pack up the test for now because pushing further is unsafe or useless.

q_02__res_L2_00_00.label: Visual & Behavioral Signals
q_02__res_L2_00_00.description: Identifying non-verbal micro-expressions and motor behaviors that distinguish agitation from cognitive struggle.

q_02__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Differentiating Agitation Micro-expressions
q_02__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Understanding specific facial cues (e.g., furrowed brow vs. blank stare) that signal emotional resistance rather than cognitive inability.

q_02__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Motor Restlessness Patterns
q_02__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Cataloging physical fidgeting or postural shifts that indicate disengagement versus physiological tremors or pain.

q_02__res_L2_00_01.label: Auditory & Verbal Markers
q_02__res_L2_00_01.description: Analyzing vocal changes that indicate a shift in patient cooperation levels during the test.

q_02__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Vocal Tone Shifts Signaling Resistance
q_02__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Identifying changes in pitch, volume, or latency that suggest the patient is becoming uncooperative rather than confused.

q_02__res_L1_01.label: Micro-Interventions for Flow Recovery
q_02__res_L1_01.description: Exploring the specific tactical adjustments OTs make to maintain assessment continuity without violating protocol standardization.

q_02__res_L2_01_00.label: Verbal De-escalation Tactics
q_02__res_L2_01_00.description: Documenting the specific language and timing used to redirect agitated patients back to the task.

q_02__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Standardized vs. Improvised Phrasing
q_02__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Determining which redirection scripts preserve test validity while effectively reducing patient agitation.

q_02__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Timing of Interjections
q_02__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Understanding the critical window for intervention before a patient fully disengages or escalates.

q_02__res_L2_01_01.label: Physical Re-anchoring
q_02__res_L2_01_01.description: Examining how physical manipulation of the environment or test materials is used to regain focus.

q_02__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Manipulating Test Materials for Focus
q_02__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Identifying how handing over cards or pointers is used to physically re-engage a distracted patient's attention.

q_02__res_L1_02.label: Real-Time Data Integrity Management
q_02__res_L1_02.description: Understanding how OTs mentally tag data quality during distress to inform automated data provenance and metadata structures.

q_02__res_L2_02_00.label: Implicit Metadata Tagging
q_02__res_L2_02_00.description: Uncovering the mental heuristics used to weight or flag specific data points collected during agitation.

q_02__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Mental Scoring of Item Validity
q_02__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Exploring how OTs internally discount specific answers given during agitation without stopping the entire session.

q_02__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Thresholds for Item vs. Session Validity
q_02__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Defining the boundary where a specific item is discarded versus when the whole assessment is compromised (distinct from termination criteria).

q_03__shared_root.label: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.
q_03__shared_root.description: Interview topic: Exploring the clinical workflow and underlying support needs of occupational therapists conducting cognitive assessments for patients.

q_03__exp_L1_00.label: Frontline Occupational Therapist
q_03__exp_L1_00.description: Tacit clinical intuition used to manage complex and unpredictable patient interactions during cognitive assessments.

q_03__exp_L2_00_00.label: Effort & Authenticity
q_03__exp_L2_00_00.description: Judging whether the patient is genuinely attempting the tasks or just guessing or looping to finish.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Spotting Genuine Effort vs. Looping
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: You can tell when they're actually straining to find the word versus just throwing out any noun or getting stuck repeating the same one to make you happy and end the test.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Consistency Across Task Types
q_03__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: If they ace the memory part but can't follow a simple one-step command right after, something doesn't add up in the data.

q_03__exp_L2_00_01.label: Contextual & Environmental Validity
q_03__exp_L2_00_01.description: Verifying if the test results match the ward reality and ensuring the environment didn't skew the data.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: The Ward Reality Check
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: If the test says they're fine but they couldn't find the bathroom an hour ago, the test is wrong, not the patient.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Fatigue Impact on Scores
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Knowing when a low score is just because they're exhausted from physio earlier, not because their cognition is gone.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Environmental Contamination
q_03__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: If the room's too loud, IV pump beeping, or someone's hovering, the data's contaminated. You have to stop.

q_03__exp_L2_00_02.label: Emotional & Physiological Safety
q_03__exp_L2_00_02.description: Identifying when frustration or physical state crosses the line into harmful reactions.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: The Shame Spiral
q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: When their face drops and they go quiet, that's not just frustration, that's shame. You have to stop before they shut down completely.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Agitation & Safety Risk
q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: If they start tapping their foot aggressively or raising their voice, you're risking a behavioral incident if you push another question.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Physiological Barriers
q_03__exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: You see them go pale, breathing changes, or wincing in pain; that's their body saying stop, not their cognition.

q_03__exp_L2_00_03.label: Engagement & Rapport
q_03__exp_L2_00_03.description: Assessing whether the patient is actively participating or just passively enduring or complying.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_03_00.label: People-Pleasing & False Compliance
q_03__exp_leaf_00_03_00.description: Some patients are so polite they'll nod along or give correct answers just to not disappoint you, even when they're completely lost or checked out.

q_03__exp_leaf_00_03_01.label: The Check-Out Moment
q_03__exp_leaf_00_03_01.description: There's a specific look where their eyes glaze over, or they start fidgeting and pulling away; pushing past that point gives you garbage data.

q_03__res_L1_00.label: Operationalizing Assessment Validity
q_03__res_L1_00.description: Understanding the criteria therapists use to distinguish between a poor cognitive score and a fundamentally flawed testing session.

q_03__res_L2_00_00.label: Thresholds for Data Trustworthiness
q_03__res_L2_00_00.description: Investigating the boundary between valid low performance and invalid test conditions.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Differentiating Low Score vs. Invalid Test
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Identify the specific behavioral cues that signal a score reflects true deficit versus testing conditions that render the data unusable.

q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Quantifying Environmental Impact on Validity
q_03__res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine at what level of environmental interference (noise, privacy breaches) the assessment data is considered compromised rather than just difficult.

q_03__res_L2_00_01.label: Qualitative Success Markers
q_03__res_L2_00_01.description: Exploring non-numerical indicators that confirm the assessment accurately captured the patient's baseline.

q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Identifying Ground Truth Indicators
q_03__res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Uncover the subtle verbal or non-verbal confirmations therapists rely on to verify the score matches their clinical intuition.

q_03__res_L1_01.label: Termination Thresholds & Safety
q_03__res_L1_01.description: Defining the specific conditions under which continuing the assessment becomes unethical, unsafe, or procedurally impossible.

q_03__res_L2_01_00.label: Physiological & Ethical Boundaries
q_03__res_L2_01_00.description: Establishing the hard limits regarding patient distress and medical stability that mandate stopping.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Defining the Ethical Point of No Return
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Clarify the specific escalation in patient agitation where adaptation strategies are no longer viable and termination is required.

q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Medical Contraindications for Testing
q_03__res_leaf_01_00_01.description: List specific physiological states (e.g., pain spikes, vitals) that act as absolute hard stops regardless of assessment progress.

q_03__res_L2_01_01.label: Protocol Integrity Breaches
q_03__res_L2_01_01.description: Determining when external interruptions violate the standardized process enough to require abortion of the session.

q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Interruption Severity Classification
q_03__res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Understand the criteria for deciding whether an interruption requires a pause, a restart, or a full session termination.

q_03__res_L1_02.label: Validity Metadata & Reporting
q_03__res_L1_02.description: Exploring how therapists communicate the reliability of a session to other clinicians through documentation.

q_03__res_L2_02_00.label: Capturing Validity Context
q_03__res_L2_02_00.description: Investigating the need for structured fields that qualify the numerical score with session reliability data.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Structured Validity Qualifiers
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Identify what specific tags or notes are needed in the EHR to indicate a score was achieved under suboptimal conditions.

q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Provenance Warnings for Future Users
q_03__res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine how to flag data in the system to warn future clinicians that this baseline may be unreliable for comparison.

q_04__exp_L1_00.label: Frontline Occupational Therapist
q_04__exp_L1_00.description: Tacit clinical intuition used to manage complex and unpredictable patient interactions during cognitive assessments.

q_04__exp_L2_00_00.label: Therapeutic Intent & Functional Truth
q_04__exp_L2_00_00.description: The core clinical objective of moving beyond standardized scores to understand what the patient can actually do in real life.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Real-Time State vs. Test Scores
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The paper says they scored 28/30, but I watched them walk into the wrong room five minutes ago. My gut says the test is lying about their 'right now' state, but I need to prove it without breaking the flow.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Emotional Safety vs. Honest Data
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: They're nodding and smiling because they don't want to disappoint me, not because they understand. I have to crack that shell to get honest data without triggering a shame spiral that shuts them down.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Split Attention on Behavioral Cues
q_04__exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: I'm administering the test but also watching for eye glazing or foot tapping. I wish I had a second set of eyes to flag when they're checking out or the environment is messing with them so I don't have to split my attention.

q_04__exp_L2_00_01.label: Workflow & Systemic Support
q_04__exp_L2_00_01.description: The administrative and systemic burdens that drain energy away from direct patient care and clinical reasoning.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Administrative Friction When Adapting
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: If I need to pause or stop because the session isn't working, I shouldn't have to fill out 10 forms. The system should just log it and let me move on without the paperwork penalty.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Protecting Clinical Flow
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: I'm deep in thought about their memory lapse and the phone rings or a nurse interrupts. Once I break that flow, I lose the subtle thread I was pulling and have to start over.

q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Time Scarcity for Deep Observation
q_04__exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: I need to catch subtle shifts in their energy to adjust in real time, but I'm always rushing. Mostly, I need more time. Always more time to just be with them.
